The court noted that a significant accounting was prepared. Mr. Tate stated that he will be doing a formal objection to the accounting, that 75% percent of the proceeds for Hodges Valley have not been deposited into a blocked account, that the accounting is incomplete and does not contain documentation indicating where the money went, and that the Elm Street property can not be insured because it is not resided in. Mr. Tate also stated that there is $180,000.00 that has gone to the attorneys, but they don't know what attorneys and for what client, that Elm Street needs to be fixed up in order to rent it, or has to be sold, and that everyone expects it to be sold at a loss and that the loss be split. Mr. Madden explained that the accounting is not finished, that it is not balanced, that he thinks it will be resolved once he gets information from his client, and that there are around 1500 pages of statements. Mr. Madden also stated that he followed what the court said and filed the accounting, that he needs more time to finish it, that he doesn't think the money exist, that he believes the money was distributed before this litigation commenced, and that his client has no interest in Elm Street. Mr. Feilders stated that he recorded the deed, ask to be allowed to distribute the property, and stated that he is confident that the loss will be very minimal. Mr. Tate stated that $800,000.00 to $900,000.00 was generated from the sale of Hodges, that the money went to Mr. Crowe and was paid to attorneys' fees, and that Mr. Crowe has to account for the money that he distributed to himself and account for what went to the attorneys. The court encourages the parties to meet and confer, and stated that they can do a drop off ex parte petition regarding the deed. The hearing date on the accounting of April 18, 2023 is continued to May 10, 2023 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204. Demurrer Of Respondent Ross Mclauran Madden To Petition Of Petitioners Patricia Callahan, Robert Callahan, And Patricia Callahan Executor Of The Estate Of Edward Callahan, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Apr-15-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom: Ross Madden in Propria Persona. David Tate for Robert Callahan and Patricia Callahan.

The Court gave a general advisement that parties present in the courtroom are adhering to social distancing and wear a face covering, that this hearing is being conducted by Zoom and the Court Call line has been activated to allow the public to listen in.

Mr. Tate stated that he understood the Court's tentative, that he appreciates that amount of time that was spent on the motion and suggested a third option where he would dismiss Mr. Madden without prejudice. The Court takes a brief recess.

The Court stated that Mr. Tate's thrid option is viable and that it is happy to add that language to the proposed form of order submitted. The Court and counsel have a brief discussion the time line for when repsponses are due and spoke about the request to consolidate the related matter.

The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the modified tentaive ruling additional the additional option for petitioner to dimiss a party without prejudice:

The moving party's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. The opposing party's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. (See Evid. Code sections 452, 453.) Judicial Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.)
The meet and confer process here failed to comply with the requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41. All parties are required to comply with the statutory meet and confer requirements going forward. However, a determination by the court that the meet and confer process was insufficient is not grounds to overrule or sustain a demurrer (CCP section 430.41(a)(4)). Based on the moving and opposing papers, the Court does not believe that ordering the parties to engage in further dialogue prior to ruling on this demurrer will result in resolution. Accordingly, the Court will rule on the merits of the demurrer.
The demurrer of Respondent Ross Madden to the Petition for Removal, Appointment, or Confirmation of Trustee; For Breach of Fiduciary Duty and Damages; to Compel Trust Accounting, etc. is SUSTAINED with leave to amend as to the entirety of the Petition. As explained below, each cause of action as currently pleaded would require pre-filing approval under Civil Code section 1714.10(a).
Petitioners argue that there is no cause of action for civil conspiracy in the Petition, and that it is therefore not subject to the strictures of Civil Code section 1714.10. However, the labels of the causes of action are not controlling; the Court must examine the allegations themselves. (Cortese v. Sherwood (2018) 26 Cal. App. 5th 445, 455, citing Berg & Berg (2005) 131 Cal.App.4th 802, 824). Cortese makes it clear that the Court must look to the substance of the claims to determine whether one or more of the causes of action is a claim "against an attorney for a civil conspiracy with his client" which arose "from any attempt to contest or compromise a claim or dispute." (Civ. Code section 1714.10(a).) Any claim falling within the ambit of section 1714.10(a) requires court preapproval unless an exception under subd. (c) applies.

The Court gave a general advisement that parties present in the courtroom are adhering to social distancing and wear a face covering, that this hearing is being conducted by Zoom and the Court Call line has been activated to allow the public to listen in.

Ms. Martinez reported that she informed the Court last night that she intended to contest to the tentative ruling and now her client would like to submit. Mr. Tate stated that he did not oppose to the court's tentative ruling.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

This motion will be heard concurrently with the demurrer of Respondent Ross Madden to the Petition for Removal, Appointment, or Confirmation of Trustee; For Breach of Fiduciary Duty and Damages; to Compel Trust Accounting, etc.
James Feilders' Motion to Quash Service of Petition is DENIED, on both the claim of lack of personal jurisdiction and on the assertion of forum non conveniens (CCP section 418.10).
Personal jurisdiction rules are the same for civil and trust proceedings, and California courts may exercise jurisdiction on any basis consistent with the state or federal Constitutions. (see Prob. Code section 17004, and CCP section 410.10.) "California courts may exercise jurisdiction to determine matters concerning trust property located in California-particularly land-even if the trust is administered elsewhere." (Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 530-31, citing Cal. Law Revision Com. com., 54A pt. 1 West's Ann. Prob. Code (2011 ed.) foll. section 17004, p. 306.)

"When a defendant moves to quash service for lack of personal jurisdiction, the plaintiff must establish by a preponderance of the evidence the facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction. The burden then shifts to the defendant to demonstrate exercising jurisdiction would be unreasonable." (Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 530, citing Jayone Foods, Inc. v. Aekyung Industrial Co. Ltd. (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 543, 553.) In seeking to meet their initial burden to show that that jurisdiction is proper here, Petitioners turn to the three-prong test in Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 531:
(1) defendants have purposefully availed themselves of forum benefits;
(2) the controversy relates to the defendants' contacts with the forum; and
(3) the exercise of jurisdiction comports with fair play and substantial justice.

"Jurisdictional facts must be proved by admissible evidence. This generally requires declarations by competent witnesses. A properly verified complaint may be treated as a declaration for this purpose." (Id. at 535, quoting Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2020) 3:387.) The Petition is verified (Pet. at pgs. 23-24) and alleges sufficient facts to address all three prongs of the test. A copy of the 2016 Trust is appended to the verified Petition herein as Exhibit A, and is incorporated by reference into the Petition.

Petitioners have satisfied their burden on prong one. Defendants purposefully avail themselves of a forum's benefits if they intentionally direct their activities at a forum such that, by virtue of the benefit the defendants receive, they should reasonably expect to be subject to jurisdiction there. (Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz (1985) 471 U.S. 462, 475-476.) By focusing on the defendants' reasonable expectations, this requirement ensures defendants will not be hailed into a jurisdiction solely because of fortuitous or attenuated contacts or because of the unilateral activity of another party. (Id. at p. 475.) (quoted by Buskirk, 53 Cal. App. 5th at 532.) "A defendant need not physically enter California at all to be subject to personal jurisdiction here." (Buskirk, supra, 53 Cal.
